bibliographic_data.title: Explicit Multi-objective Model Predictive Control for Nonlinear Systems Under Uncertainty
bibliographic_data.abstract: In real-world problems, uncertainties (e.g., errors in the measurement, precision errors) often lead to poor performance of numerical algorithms when not explicitly taken into account. This is also the case for control problems, where optimal solutions can degrade in quality or even become infeasible. Thus, there is the need to design methods that can handle uncertainty. In this work, we consider nonlinear multi-objective optimal control problems with uncertainty on the initial conditions, and in particular their incorporation into a feedback loop via model predictive control (MPC). In multi-objective optimal control, an optimal compromise between multiple conflicting criteria has to be found. For such problems, not much has been reported in terms of uncertainties. To address this problem class, we design an offline/online framework to compute an approximation of efficient control strategies. This approach is closely related to explicit MPC for nonlinear systems, where the potentially expensive optimization problem is solved in an offline phase in order to enable fast solutions in the online phase. In order to reduce the numerical cost of the offline phase – which grows exponentially with the parameter dimension – we exploit symmetries in the control problems. Furthermore, in order to ensure optimality of the solutions, we include an additional online optimization step, which is considerably cheaper than the original multi-objective optimization problem. We test our framework on a car maneuvering problem where safety and speed are the objectives. The multi-objective framework allows for online adaptations of the desired objective. Alternatively, an automatic scalarizing procedure yields very efficient feedback controls. Our results show that the method is capable of designing driving strategies that deal better with uncertainties in the initial conditions, which translates into potentially safer and faster driving strategies.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes an offline/online framework for explicit multi-objective model predictive control (MPC) to handle uncertainties in initial conditions for nonlinear systems, which is a novel approach in the context of multi-objective optimal control.
core_content.key_methodology: The authors design an offline phase where the optimization problem is solved for many different initial conditions and an online phase where solutions are selected from a library. Symmetries in the control problems are exploited to reduce computational complexity, and an additional online optimization step ensures optimality of the solutions.
outcomes: Developed a framework for handling uncertainties in initial conditions using explicit MPC for nonlinear systems
Exploited symmetries in control problems to reduce computational cost
Ensured optimality through an additional online optimization step
core_content.full_text_markdown: In real-world engineering problems, multiple objectives need to be optimized concurrently. This leads to multi-objective optimization problems (MOPs) where the goal is to identify the set of optimal trade-off solutions (the Pareto set). In control systems, an input function must be computed such that a dynamical system behaves optimally with respect to these multiple objectives. Model predictive control (MPC) is a successful approach for feedback control with conflicting criteria, solving an open-loop optimization problem repeatedly over a finite horizon and applying the solution directly to the real system. However, in multi-objective cases, special measures are needed due to the need for solutions that can be computed in real-time. Explicit MPC involves pre-computing a library of optimal inputs offline, which is then used online. This paper addresses uncertainties in initial conditions, a common issue in control systems where sensor measurements might be inaccurate. The authors propose an offline/online framework for explicit multi-objective MPC to handle such uncertainties. In the offline phase, they solve optimization problems for many different initial conditions and exploit symmetries to reduce computational complexity. An additional online optimization step ensures that solutions remain optimal even after interpolation between library entries. The method is tested on a car maneuvering problem where safety and speed are the objectives, demonstrating its capability to design driving strategies robust to uncertainties in initial conditions.
